explanation: A gerund is a verbal noun: it is derived from a verb but functions as a noun. E.g. amandī = of loving, amandō = by loving. It only exists in the genitive, dative, accusative, and ablative singular.

translation: The students were eager for reading.
explanation: legendī is genitive of the gerund of legō (to read). Cupidus + genitive: eager for reading. Gerund genitive = stem + -andī (1st conj.) or -endī (other conj.).
scrībendō is the dative/ablative of the gerund of scrībō (to write). Here ablative of means: by/through writing. Dative/ablative gerund = -andō (1st) or -endō (others).
A gerundive is a verbal adjective (agrees with a noun in case, number, gender) expressing that something must be done. E.g. amanda = loveable / to be loved (fem. nom. sg.).
dēlenda est = must be destroyed. The gerundive dēlenda (feminine, agreeing with Rōma) + est expresses necessity. This is the gerundive of obligation construction.
legendī sunt = must be read. Librī is masculine plural; the gerundive legendī (masculine plural) agrees with it. legendī + sunt = must be read.

translation: The soldiers were ready for fighting.
explanation: pugnandum is accusative of the gerund after ad: ad pugnandum = for the purpose of fighting. Ad + accusative gerund is a common Latin way of expressing purpose.

sentence: Gerund vs gerundive: gerund is a _____, gerundive is a _____.
blank_word: gerund is a noun; gerundive is an adjective
display: Gerund vs gerundive: gerund is a _____, gerundive is a _____.
options: gerund is a noun; gerundive is an adjective
gerund is an adjective; gerundive is a noun
gerund is active; gerundive is passive
correct: gerund is a noun; gerundive is an adjective
explanation: Gerund = verbal noun (no gender/number agreement). Gerundive = verbal adjective (agrees with a noun in case, number, gender, often expressing necessity).
cōnsulendum est = it must be consulted, i.e. the senators must be consulted. Impersonal gerundive construction: gerundive (neuter nom. sg.) + est; the dative (senātōribus) shows by whom.
